Location & Data

Rare Surnames in America: Uncommon Family Names

Discover the rarest surnames in America, from nearly extinct family names to unusual surnames with fewer than 100 bearers nationwide.

By Ziven Borceg9 min readFebruary 18, 2026

While most Americans share one of the 150,000+ recorded surnames, some family names are so rare that fewer than 100 people carry them. These surnames represent unique family histories, immigration stories, and linguistic evolution.

What Makes a Surname Rare?

A surname is considered rare in America when:

  • Fewer than 1,000 people carry it nationwide
  • It appears in fewer than 5 states
  • It has no variant spellings in common use

    • The rarest surnames often belong to:

  • Recent immigrants whose names haven't spread
  • Families with unusual spelling changes at Ellis Island
  • Very small families with few descendants
  • Names from languages with limited US presence

    • Categories of Rare American Surnames

    Modified Immigration Names

  • Pfistershammer — German origin, fewer than 50 bearers
  • Szczyrbak — Polish, under 100 bearers
  • Bhattacharyya — Bengali, rare due to complex spelling

    • Anglicized Rarities

  • Miracle — Possibly from French 'Miracel,' under 3,000 bearers
  • Twelvetrees — English, under 200 bearers
  • Tumbledown — English, virtually extinct

    • Indigenous Names

    Many Native American surnames are extremely rare due to small tribal populations. These names carry particular cultural significance and heritage.

    The Most Common vs. Rarest

    | Rank | Most Common | Count | Rarest Examples | Count |
    |------|-----------|-------|----------------|-------|
    | 1 | Smith | 2.5M | Zzyzx | <10 |
    | 2 | Johnson | 2.0M | Quiddington | <20 |
    | 3 | Williams | 1.6M | Fernsby | <50 |
    | 4 | Brown | 1.4M | Vileli | <30 |
    | 5 | Jones | 1.4M | Miracel | <100 |

    Preserving Rare Surnames

    Many rare surnames face extinction. Genealogists and family historians work to document these names before they disappear, creating records that connect future generations to their heritage.

    Check How Many People Have This Name
